aliases: 纳西当冈 (Nà Xī Dāng Gāng)

chinese: 纳西当冈 (Nà Xī Dāng Gāng)

region_of_origin: Kelantan and Terengganu, Malaysia; consumed in Singapore by the Kelantanese Malay community
food_subgroup: Mixed glutinous and regular rice cooked in coconut milk with fenugreek, served with ikan tongkol (skipjack tuna) curry, pickled vegetables, and hard-boiled egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Nasi Dagang. Closest parent would be a coconut milk rice dish (FOODON:03315763 — rice dish), but no exact match exists for this Malay east coast preparation.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dish — closest FoodEx2 parent code for cooked rice-based composite dish; no specific Nasi Dagang code exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Nasi Dagang. A00LC (rice dish, composite) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Nasi Dagang or equivalent Malay rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Nasi Dagang is approximately 350g, comprising a mound of coconut milk rice (approximately 200g cooked rice) served with ikan tongkol curry (approximately 100g fish and sauce), pickled vegetables, and a hard-boiled egg.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Dagang — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO-INFOODS SEA Food Composition Data
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nasi Dagang is listed as a composite dish in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Values represent the full dish including rice, coconut milk, and ikan tongkol curry components per 100g edible portion. FAO/INFOODS SEA tables used for cross-validation of micronutrient values.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice-Based Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; White rice (GI 72) and glutinous rice (GI 98) composite estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (www.glycemicindex.com); peer-reviewed literature on glutinous rice GI
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nasi Dagang as a composite dish. GI estimated as ~72 based on a 60:40 blend of regular white rice (GI 72, University of Sydney GI Database) and white glutinous rice (GI ~98, peer-reviewed literature). The fenugreek seeds (halba) in the rice provide a modest GI-lowering effect via galactomannan fibre and 4-hydroxyisoleucine insulinotropic activity, partially offsetting the high-GI glutinous rice component. Composite GI range estimated at 72–82. GL per 350g serving calculated as: (GI 72 × 63g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 45.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 700mg per 350g serving (200mg per 100g); primary sources are ikan tongkol curry sauce and cooking salt
hypertension.guidance: At 700mg sodium per serving, Nasi Dagang contributes approximately 30–35% of the 2,000mg daily sodium target for hypertensive patients; portion control and requesting reduced salt in the curry preparation are recommended strategies.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load (GL 45.4 per serving) from the 60:40 regular-to-glutinous rice blend providing approximately 63g available carbohydrate per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The GL of 45.4 per standard serving is very high for T2DM management; patients should reduce rice portion by 30–40%, noting that the fenugreek seeds (halba) provide a clinically documented but insufficient GI-lowering effect via galactomannan fibre and 4-hydroxyisoleucine to offset the large carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 12.6g per serving (3.6g per 100g) from coconut milk, partially offset by omega-3 DHA approximately 300mg per serving from ikan tongkol (skipjack tuna)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat from coconut milk (12.6g per serving) is a CVD concern, but the ikan tongkol provides significant cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (approximately 300mg DHA per serving); overall CVD risk is moderate and lower than Nasi Lemak, which typically contains more coconut milk per gram of rice.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ikan tongkol (skipjack tuna) provides approximately 300mg DHA per serving with significant anti-inflammatory benefit; turmeric curcumin bioavailability enhanced by coconut milk fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Nasi Dagang with ikan tongkol is one of the better traditional Malay rice dishes for RA patients due to its omega-3 content; the coconut milk fat also improves curcumin absorption from the turmeric in the curry paste.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Dagang (literally 'trader's rice') is the iconic breakfast dish of Malaysia's east coast, originating from Kelantan and Terengganu states. It is prepared by cooking a blend of regular white rice and glutinous rice (typically in a 60:40 or 70:30 ratio) together in coconut milk with fenugreek seeds (halba) and shallots, producing a fragrant, slightly sticky, and richly flavoured rice. The dish is served with ikan tongkol (skipjack tuna) curry — a robust, spiced curry paste containing turmeric, chilli, lemongrass, and galangal — along with pickled cucumber and carrot (acar) and a hard-boiled egg. The texture is denser and stickier than Nasi Lemak due to the glutinous rice component, and the flavour profile is more complex, with the fenugreek seeds imparting a distinctive slightly bitter, maple-like aroma. Clinically, Nasi Dagang has a better saturated fat profile than Nasi Lemak (less coconut milk per gram of rice, approximately 3.6g saturated fat per 100g versus 5–6g for Nasi Lemak). The ikan tongkol provides approximately 300mg DHA omega-3 per serving, making it one of the better traditional Malay rice dishes for cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefit. The fenugreek seeds contain galactomannan fibre and 4-hydroxyisoleucine, which have documented glucose-modulating and insulinotropic activity in human trials. However, the high glycemic load (GL 45.4 per 350g serving) from the mixed rice portion remains the primary concern for T2DM patients. Nasi Dagang is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is less commonly available in Singapore than Nasi Lemak but has growing presence at Malay food stalls and is consumed by the Kelantanese Malay community in Singapore, particularly at breakfast and during Hari Raya celebrations.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Malaysian Food Composition Database), which is the primary approved regional source for Malaysian dishes, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia tables. Nasi Dagang is listed as a named composite dish in the MyFCD. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for white rice and peer-reviewed glutinous rice GI data, with documented fenugreek modulation effect. Confidence set to high as the primary approved source contains an exact dish match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Nasi Dagang as a composite dish are not available in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in the primary source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per IMR Malaysia MyFCD — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). GI is an analogous estimate: no direct GI measurement of Nasi Dagang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; the value of 72 is derived from a weighted composite of regular white rice (GI 72) and glutinous rice (GI ~98) in a 60:40 ratio, with a modest downward adjustment for fenugreek galactomannan fibre effect. GL per serving calculated as (72 × 63g available carbohydrate per 350g serving) / 100 = 45.4. Sodium per serving (700mg) calculated as 200mg/100g × 350g. Saturated fat per serving (12.6g) calculated as 3.6g/100g × 350g. The food_group in the input JSON was incorrectly listed as 'Malay Heritage Kuih & Desserts'; corrected to 'Grains and staples' as Nasi Dagang is a savoury rice dish, not a dessert or kuih.
